title: [Question] - Access NFS Home Dir w/ keep-id
body:
Sorry for not filling out all the info, but this is really more of a question

**podman version:**
Version:      2.0.2
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.13.11
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64

**System Info:**
selinux is enforcing
home directories are NFS mounted

Out of the following two commands
1) podman container run -it --rm -v $HOME:$HOME --entrypoint bash --security-opt label=disable  _image_
2) podman container run -it --rm -v $HOME:$HOME --entrypoint bash --security-opt label=disable  --userns keep-id  _image_

Result:
1) Work great, but I do not have access to user's group info
2) Error's out:
~~~
Error: container_linux.go:349: starting container process caused "process_linux.go:449: container init caused \"rootfs_linux.go:58: moun
ting \\\"/home/user1\\\" to rootfs \\\"/var/lib/users/user1/containers/storage/overlay/8cf9b03f94fbfcfafe4150092b5aff74b58dbb6
62fda50cebe4fe72721766798/merged\\\" at \\\"/home/user1\\\" caused \\\"stat /home/user1: permission denied\\\"\"": OCI runtime
 permission denied error
~~~

I'm gonna take a stab this has something to do with the /etc/sub?id files and that the remote NFS server has no concept of the sub?id(s).

My question is can an OCI hook get me around this, or am I dead in the water?
If I'm dead  in the water, suggestion on the best way to get group into into the container?

comments.body:
If you are using crun, you can leak the groups into the container.

```
man podman run
...
       Note:  if  the  user  only has access rights via a group, accessing the device from inside a rootless container will fail. The crun(1) runtime offers a workaround for this by adding the option --annotation run.oci.keep_origi‐
       nal_groups=1.

```

Did you tell container/storage to ignore-chown-errors?
I did not on the ignore chown errors, and the system I'm on has the runc environment as opposed to the crun environment

The CHOWN Errors should not matter whether it is in runc or crun mode.  
On and NFS Homedir, the NFS Server will not allow a process in the user namespace to chown a file to a different UID/GID, since it has no concept of User Namespace.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@choman did leaking the group via crun solve the issue for you?
If it did not, reopen this issue.
